title: Perfect-bound notebook
subtitle: A spine-glued binding with a wrap-around card cover
excerpt: Perfect binding glues a stack of loose sheets directly at the spine into a wrap-around cover. No sewing, no signatures: the pages hold by a thick layer of flexible PVA. The result opens flat from the first page and is how most commercial notebooks and paperbacks are made.
sourceNotes: The adhesive-only perfect binding method, developed commercially from the 1930s onward, is widely documented in bookbinding manuals from the mid-twentieth century onward. No single pre-1928 PD source covers the modern PVA variant; the technique here synthesises the standard workshop method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding method in which a stack of loose leaves is held at the spine by a thick layer of adhesive, with a cover wrapped directly around the spine.
The adhesive applied to the trimmed spine edge of a perfect-bound text block. Flexible PVA is the standard; hot-melt adhesive is the commercial equivalent.

content.text: In a
perfect-bound
book, all the pages are held solely by the
spine glue

. The spine edge must be roughened so the adhesive can penetrate between the sheets, and the glue coat must be thick enough to grip but flexible enough to allow the book to open without cracking. This version produces an A5 notebook from 100 loose sheets (200 pages) in a wrap-around card cover.
What you need

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 perfect-bound notebook

items.name: Text paper, 90 gsm, A5, grain-short (grain perpendicular to spine)
Cover card, 300 gsm, cut to A5 height × (two A5 widths plus spine width)
Flexible acid-free PVA (undiluted)
items.qty: Enough for 2 to 3 coats across the spine width

items.name: Binder clips or book press
items.qty: To hold the stack during gluing

attrs.heading: Grain direction
attrs.body: For perfect binding, the text-paper grain runs perpendicular to the spine so pages flex away from the binding, not toward it. A grain-parallel text block will crack at the spine.

content.text: Preparing the text block
Stack all 100 sheets in order. Tap the stack on a flat surface to align the spine edge and head edge as squarely as possible.
Clamp the stack between two boards cut to the exact page size, aligned flush at the spine edge. The clamp must hold the pages rigid during gluing; use binder clips at each corner, or a book press.

Roughen the spine edge with coarse sandpaper, working along the spine in short strokes. This abrades the paper fibres and opens channels for the PVA to enter between sheets. The spine will look slightly fuzzy; that is correct.
Gluing the spine

Apply a first thin coat of undiluted flexible PVA across the entire spine edge, working it into the gaps between sheets with the brush or a scrap of card. Let this first coat dry for 15 minutes until it is no longer tacky.
Apply a second thicker coat, again working PVA into the spine edge. This is the structural coat. Allow it to dry for 30 minutes. The spine should feel firm and slightly springy when pressed.
Apply a third thin coat to act as the adhesive for the cover. Do not let this coat dry before wrapping the cover.
Preparing and wrapping the cover

Measure the spine thickness of the clamped text block. Score and fold the cover card to produce a spine panel of that exact width, with a back cover of A5 width on one side and a front cover of A5 width on the other. Both fold lines should be scored with the bone folder against the ruler before folding.
While the third PVA coat is still wet, remove the clamps and press the spine edge down onto the inner face of the cover's spine panel. Close both cover panels around the text block.
Re-clamp the book, now including the cover, and press under weight for at least 2 hours.
Finishing and care

Once dry, the cover should be trimmed at the foredge to flush with the text block if any overhang remains. Perfect-bound books open well but do not open fully flat; the first and last pages see the most stress from opening. Avoid forcing the cover back past 180 degrees.
Variations

Fan binding: before the third PVA coat, fan the clamped pages slightly side to side (bending the whole stack, not just the top page) so PVA coats the very edge of each individual sheet. This gives more per-page adhesion and a stronger bind for papers over 100 gsm.
